title: Dog Diarrhea with Fresh Blood Streaks: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your dog has fresh blood in their diarrhea, whether it is an emergency, and why taking a photo of the stool helps your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bright red blood in dog stool (hematochezia) usually indicates lower gastrointestinal irritation and is typically a medium urgency, but requires immediate emergency care if accompanied by vomiting or lethargy. Taking a photo of the stool is a vital triage step that helps your veterinarian accurately assess the severity of the condition.

What does it mean if there is bright red blood in my dog's stool?

Finding bright red streaks of fresh blood in your dog's diarrhea can be frightening. In veterinary medicine, this is called hematochezia. Unlike dark, tarry stool which indicates bleeding in the upper digestive tract, bright red blood typically suggests inflammation or irritation in the lower gastrointestinal tract, specifically the colon or rectum.

When should I take my dog to the emergency vet for bloody stool?

  • The urgency level for fresh blood streaks is typically Medium.
  • If your dog is alert and behaving normally, schedule a veterinary exam within 24 hours.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if the diarrhea is accompanied by persistent vomiting, extreme lethargy, or pale gums.
  • Emergency care is required if the stool consists purely of blood with no fecal matter.

How does taking a photo of my dog's stool help with the triage process?

  • Taking a clear photo provides your veterinarian with objective data regarding the volume of blood and the presence of mucus.
  • A photo captures the specific stool color, which helps the medical team determine the severity of the inflammation.
  • This visual evidence allows for more accurate prioritization of your dog's care compared to a verbal description alone.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Acute onset of profuse hemorrhagic diarrhea, often resembling raspberry jam, is characteristic of hemorrhagic gastroenteritis (HGE), particularly in small or toy breed dogs. Common clinical findings include vomiting, anorexia, lethargy, and abdominal pain; vomiting may precede the bloody diarrhea. Marked peracute fluid loss can rapidly lead to hypovolemic shock. Diagnosis is typically based on signalment, acute clinical signs, and hemoconcentration (PCV ≥55%) with normal to slightly decreased total plasma protein concentration. Selective fecal cultures for pathogens, such as Clostridium spp, Salmonella spp, and Campylobacter spp, and evaluation for Clostridium spp enterotoxin via fecal ELISA can be considered. While abnormalities on CBC are usually limited to hemoconcentration and neutrophilic leukocytosis, neutropenia should raise suspicion for sepsis or parvovirus enteritis. In large bowel diarrhea, fresh blood (hematochezia) may be present in loose to semi-formed feces, without melena.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 396)

faqs.question: Can a change in diet cause blood in dog stool?
faqs.answer: Yes, sudden dietary changes or 'dietary indiscretion' (eating something they shouldn't) can cause acute colitis, leading to inflammation and fresh blood streaks.

faqs.question: Should I feed my dog if they have bloody diarrhea?
faqs.answer: It is often recommended to offer a bland diet like boiled chicken and white rice, but you should consult your veterinarian before feeding to see if a short fast is better for your dog's specific case.

faqs.question: Are parasites a common cause of blood in diarrhea?
faqs.answer: Yes, intestinal parasites like hookworms or whipworms, as well as protozoa like Giardia, are frequent causes of bloody stools in dogs.
